insight: Cryo Ray's targeting is both its strength and weakness compared to Gauss Cannon: you can choose its target (advantage), but you also cannot choose its target in some contexts (disadvantage). The value depends on which units you want to hit.
author: consensus
context: Community members made complementary observations about Cryo Ray vs Gauss Cannon target selection in the same thread.

insight: Deadeye is often bought primarily as an economic unit rather than a combat unit — it is purchased because it is more resource-efficient than buying Engineers, making its role closer to a 'weird type of drone' than a traditional attacker.
author: megasupp
context: Discussion about Deadeye's in-game role and why it gets purchased, leading to analysis of its economic versus combat value.

insight: Asymmetric Deadeye games (where only one player goes Deadeyes) are rare but more viable than commonly believed. Deadeyes can both beat and lose to Tarsiers depending on precise timing — the window is extremely narrow, described as 'balanced on a knife's edge' where half a turn changes the outcome.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers pushed back on the assumption that Deadeye sets are always symmetric or always lose to Tarsiers, citing timing sensitivity.

insight: A first-turn P1 Fastimus (fast Animus) beats P1's Deadeye rushes in base set. The specific Deadeye rush line that wins versus Tarsiers is not relevant to real games because it loses to being mirrored.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion of the asymmetric Deadeye vs Tarsier matchup and whether specific Deadeye rush lines are practically viable.

insight: When symmetric Deadeye games occur, the game is effectively put on hold for approximately 4 turns as both players drone-kill each other, making the gameplay repetitive and requiring memorization of the correct line.
author: iminabearsuit
context: Discussion about whether Deadeye is boring to play with, with multiple community members agreeing on the stalemate dynamic.

insight: Deadeye is theoretically a good counter to Wincer and to high-economy units like Centurion, as it can remove the drones that power those strategies.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ proposed specific matchup contexts where Deadeye has strategic value beyond its baseline economic role.

insight: Base+1 viability (whether a unit is good in a set containing only the base set plus that one unit) is the best available proxy for overall unit strength, despite its limitations such as not accounting for units that benefit P2 more than P1.
author: velizar_
context: Discussion about how to identify underpowered units deserving buffs, with megasupp noting the metric is somewhat misleading for P2-favored units.

insight: The Fuge unit (event/custom unit) was considered completely broken: P1 T1 Fuge was assessed as an autowin in practically every set, prompting calls for an emergency hotfix. The unit was reportedly cheaper than an 11-cost BT3 equivalent.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 and msven independently flagged Fuge as overpowered during an event playtest, with masn6811 claiming to have verified it across multiple sets.

insight: Arms Race at cost 6 was considered potentially degenerate/too strong by multiple players; the prior 9/3 version was seen as more balanced. silentslayers noted that Arms Race already performs its intended role well and did not need a buff.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of proposed balance changes for an event patch, with msven, masn6811, and silentslayers all weighing in on Arms Race.

insight: The proposed Ferritin buff (BT1 version converting 3R to 3B) was controversial: the BT2 version was considered slightly weak, but the main problem is that BT2 Ferritin does not help natural Animus against Fastimus and actually benefits Fastimus against natural Animus. A 3R->3B BT1 version was proposed as more fair than the buff under discussion.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 analyzed Ferritin's asymmetric interaction with Fastimus vs natural Animus builds during an event balance discussion.

insight: In inflation-based unit valuation models, a drone's value (f) equals the sum of an infinite geometric series of its future production: 1 + 1/R + 1/R^2 + ..., where R is the inflation rate. At i=1/3 (inflation of 33%), f equals exactly 4. This framework can be extended to evaluate any unit producing resources each turn.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers explained the mathematical model developed by xnor_ for calculating unit economic value using recursive inflation, crediting mtanzer for teaching this framework.

insight: When using inflation calculations to evaluate Prismata units, fixing a specific BV (blue value) is less informative than keeping BV as a variable. Comparing break-even curves in inflation-BV space across different units or trades reveals which option dominates under which conditions.
author: apooche
context: apooche responded to masn6811's suggestion to fix BV=2.7 for calculations, arguing the variable approach yields more strategic insight.

insight: Historical Drake versions at 14BBB and 15BBB were massively P2-favored, 16BBB was massively P1-favored, and 17BBB was too weak. The 15BBB version was considered the most fun despite being unbalanceable. Omega Splitter at 13BBB was intentionally not nerfed further to avoid making it strictly worse than the 14BBB Drake.
author: masn6811
context: Prismata veterans discussed historical Drake balance iterations and how Omega Splitter's cost was set relative to Drake's old versions.

insight: Master Bot can get stuck in a pattern where it never buys any attackers because it must spend all resources on defense, effectively stalling indefinitely without making offensive progress.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ mentioned experiencing bot games where Master Bot was permanently locked into pure defense with no attacker purchases.

insight: Master Bot failed to recognize that clicking Ebb allows the resources generated to be used to purchase additional Drones the same turn, missing a basic resource-chaining interaction.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ shared a replay showing Master Bot missing the Ebb-into-Drone purchase optimization, describing it sarcastically as 'the bot discovers the hack'.
